question.subject: cs
question.stem: Convert the 8-bit binary number 01101010 to denary. Show your working.

mark_scheme.description: States the place values for an 8-bit number

student_answer: Place values are 128 64 32 16 8 4 2 1. The 1s are at 64, 32, 8 and 2. 64+32+8+2 = 106.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: State the worst-case time complexity of a linear search over n items and justify your answer.
question.model_answer: O(n) because in the worst case every one of the n items is checked.
question.working: Worst case is the target absent or last; the loop inspects all n elements, so cost grows linearly with n.

mark_scheme.description: Justifies with worst case checking all n items

student_answer: It is O(n).

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Compare a stack and a queue. Give the order each uses, and one real use of each.
question.model_answer: A stack is LIFO; a queue is FIFO. A stack suits undo history; a queue suits a print spooler.
question.working: Stack: last item pushed is first popped (LIFO). Queue: first item enqueued is first dequeued (FIFO). Uses: stack for call/undo stacks, queue for job/print scheduling.

mark_scheme.description: Gives a valid use of a stack
Gives a valid use of a queue

student_answer: A stack is LIFO and a queue is FIFO. A stack can be used for an undo feature in an editor.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Define what a primary key is in a relational database table.
question.model_answer: An attribute (or set of attributes) that uniquely identifies each record in a table.
question.working: A primary key must be unique per row and cannot be null, so it identifies exactly one record.

mark_scheme.description: Identifies it as an attribute/field of the table
States it uniquely identifies each record/row

student_answer: It is the most important column in the table where you usually start.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Describe the purpose of a compiler and give one advantage of compiled code over interpreted code.
question.model_answer: A compiler translates whole source code into machine code before execution; compiled code runs faster as it is not translated each run.
question.working: Compiler: source -> object/machine code in one pass ahead of run time. Advantage: faster execution and no translator needed at run time.

mark_scheme.description: States a compiler translates source code to machine code
States it translates the whole program before execution
Gives a valid advantage (e.g. faster execution)

student_answer: A compiler turns the source code into machine code all at once before it runs, and the compiled program then runs faster because it does not need translating again.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Convert the denary number 50 to an 8-bit binary number. Show your working.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Convert the hexadecimal number 2F to denary. Show your working.

mark_scheme.description: Converts each hex digit to its value (2 and 15)

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Convert the denary number 200 to hexadecimal. Show your working.
question.model_answer: C8
question.working: 200 / 16 = 12 remainder 8. 12 = C, remainder 8 -> C8.

mark_scheme.description: Converts 12 to the hex digit C

student_answer: 200 / 16 = 12 remainder 8. 12 is C, so the answer is C8.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Add the 8-bit binary numbers 00101101 and 00010110. Show your working.
question.working: 45 + 22 = 67 = 01000011. Column addition with carries gives the same.

mark_scheme.description: Aligns the two numbers by place value

student_answer: I lined them up and added each column but lost track of one of the carries, getting 00111011.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Represent -25 as an 8-bit two's complement binary number. Show your working.

student_answer: 25 is 00011001. Flip the bits to get 11100110 then add one to get 11100111.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Explain what is meant by an overflow error in binary addition.
question.model_answer: When the result of an addition needs more bits than are available, so the carry out of the most significant bit is lost and the stored result is wrong.
question.working: Fixed bit width cannot hold the true result; the extra high-order bit is discarded.

mark_scheme.description: States the result is too large for the available bits
States this gives an incorrect/lost result

student_answer: It happens when the answer to a sum is too big to fit in the number of bits, so a bit is lost and the answer stored is wrong.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Perform a logical left shift of two places on 00001011 and state the effect on the denary value.
question.model_answer: 00101100, which multiplies the value by 4.
question.working: Shift left 2 -> 00101100. Each left shift multiplies by 2, so two shifts multiply by 4.

mark_scheme.description: Performs a left shift (not a right shift)
States the value is multiplied by 4

student_answer: Shifting two places gives 00000010, and a shift like this multiplies the number by 4.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: ASCII uses 7 bits per character. Explain how this limits the characters that can be represented.
question.model_answer: 7 bits give 2^7 = 128 different patterns, so only 128 distinct characters can be encoded.
question.working: Number of patterns = 2^bits = 2^7 = 128 characters maximum.

mark_scheme.description: Links this to a maximum of 128 characters

student_answer: With 7 bits you get 2^7 = 128 combinations, so ASCII can only represent 128 different characters.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Give one advantage of Unicode over ASCII and explain why.
question.model_answer: Unicode can represent far more characters because it uses more bits, so it supports many languages and symbols (e.g. emoji).
question.working: More bits -> more patterns -> larger character set covering global scripts.

student_answer: Unicode can store many more characters than ASCII and so it can represent different alphabets and symbols from many languages.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: An image is 100 by 200 pixels with a colour depth of 4 bits per pixel. Calculate the file size in bytes. Show your working.
question.model_answer: 10000 bytes

mark_scheme.description: Divides bits by 8 to convert to bytes

student_answer: 100 x 200 = 20000 pixels. 20000 x 4 = 80000, so the file size is 80000 bytes.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: State three factors that affect the file size of a digital sound recording.
question.model_answer: Sample rate, bit depth (sample resolution) and the duration of the recording.
question.working: Larger sample rate, higher bit depth and longer duration all increase the number of stored bits.

student_answer: The sample rate and the bit depth both affect the size of the file.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Which of these file formats uses lossy compression? A) PNG B) JPEG C) BMP D) TXT
question.model_answer: B) JPEG
question.working: JPEG discards image detail to reduce size, which is lossy compression.

student_answer: B

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Explain how run length encoding (RLE) reduces the size of a file.
question.model_answer: It replaces runs of repeated values with a single value and a count of how many times it repeats, storing fewer items.
question.working: e.g. AAAA -> 4A: one count + one value instead of four characters.

mark_scheme.description: States repeated values are replaced by a value and a count
Explains this stores fewer items / less data

student_answer: It looks for characters that repeat and stores them in a shorter way.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Describe how a linear search finds a target value in a list.
question.model_answer: Start at the first item and compare each item in turn with the target until a match is found or the end of the list is reached.
question.working: Sequential check of every element from start to end; stop on match or exhaustion.

mark_scheme.description: Compares each item in turn with the target
Stops when found or end of list reached

student_answer: You start at the first element and check each one in order against the value you want, stopping when you find it or you run out of items.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Describe how a binary search works and state one requirement of the list it searches.
question.model_answer: Check the middle item; if it is the target stop, otherwise discard the half that cannot contain it and repeat on the remaining half. The list must be sorted.
question.working: Halving the search space each step; precondition is an ordered list.

mark_scheme.description: Discards the half that cannot contain the target
States the list must be sorted/ordered

student_answer: You look at the middle item, then throw away the half it can't be in and repeat the process on what's left until you find it.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Describe how a bubble sort orders a list of numbers into ascending order.
question.model_answer: Compare adjacent pairs and swap them if they are in the wrong order; repeat passes through the list until a pass makes no swaps.
question.working: Largest values 'bubble' to the end each pass; terminate when a full pass has no swaps.

mark_scheme.description: Swaps them if in the wrong order
Repeats in passes through the list
Stops when a pass makes no swaps

student_answer: It compares two items next to each other and swaps them if they are the wrong way round, then keeps going through the list doing more passes.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Describe how a merge sort orders a list of numbers.
question.model_answer: Repeatedly split the list into halves until each sublist has one item, then merge sublists back together in order to produce a single sorted list.
question.working: Divide-and-conquer: split to singletons, then merge pairs in order.

mark_scheme.description: Splits the list into smaller sublists
Continues splitting until sublists have one item

student_answer: A merge sort goes through the list comparing each item to the one next to it and swapping any that are out of order until the whole list is sorted.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: State the worst-case time complexity of a binary search and justify it.
question.model_answer: O(log n) because each comparison halves the number of items still to be searched.
question.working: Halving the search space gives log2(n) comparisons in the worst case.

mark_scheme.description: Justifies with halving the search space each step

student_answer: It is O(log n) because every step halves how many items are left to look through.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: State what is meant by an array.
question.model_answer: An ordered collection of items of the same data type, accessed by an index.
question.working: Fixed-size, same-type, index-addressed collection.

mark_scheme.description: Collection of items of the same data type

student_answer: A data structure that stores several values of the same type together, each found by its index.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: A 2D array grid is indexed [row][column] starting at 0. The first row is [4, 9, 2, 7]. State the value of grid[0][2] and explain why.
question.model_answer: 2, because column index 2 is the third item in row 0.
question.working: Row 0 = [4,9,2,7]; index 2 (zero-based) is the value 2.

mark_scheme.description: Explains zero-based indexing selects the third item

student_answer: grid[0][2] is 9.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: A stack is empty. The operations push(A), push(B), pop(), push(C) are carried out. State the item returned by pop() and the final contents of the stack from bottom to top.
question.model_answer: pop() returns B; final stack is A, C (bottom to top).
question.working: Push A,B -> [A,B]. Pop removes top B. Push C -> [A,C].

mark_scheme.description: Final stack contains A and C

student_answer: pop() returns B because it was on the top. The stack then has A at the bottom and C on the top.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: A queue is empty. The operations enqueue(X), enqueue(Y), dequeue(), enqueue(Z) are carried out. State the item returned by dequeue() and the final contents of the queue from front to back.
question.model_answer: dequeue() returns X; final queue is Y, Z (front to back).
question.working: Enqueue X,Y -> [X,Y]. Dequeue removes front X. Enqueue Z -> [Y,Z].

mark_scheme.description: Recognises X was at the front (FIFO)
Final queue is Y then Z front to back

student_answer: dequeue() gives back X. After that the queue holds Y and Z.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: In a database table, define the terms 'record' and 'field'.
question.model_answer: A record is one complete row of related data; a field is a single column/attribute within a record.
question.working: Row = record; column = field.

mark_scheme.description: Record is a row / one entity's data
Field is a column / single attribute

student_answer: A record is one row holding all the data about one thing, and a field is a single column such as one attribute.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: A table Students has fields Name and Year. Write an SQL statement that returns the Name of every student.
question.model_answer: SELECT Name FROM Students;
question.working: SELECT the Name column FROM the Students table.

mark_scheme.description: Uses SELECT with the Name field

student_answer: SELECT Name FROM Students;

question.subject: cs
question.stem: A table Students has fields Name and Year. Write an SQL statement that returns the Name of every student in Year 11.
question.model_answer: SELECT Name FROM Students WHERE Year = 11;
question.working: Add a WHERE clause filtering Year = 11.

mark_scheme.description: Includes a WHERE clause on Year
Correct condition Year = 11 with valid syntax

student_answer: SELECT Name FROM Students WHERE Year = 11 and nothing else needed

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Explain what a foreign key is and what it is used for.
question.model_answer: A foreign key is a field in one table that refers to the primary key of another table, used to link the two tables.
question.working: FK = PK of another table held as a field; creates a relationship.

mark_scheme.description: A field referring to the primary key of another table

student_answer: It is a key that is used in a table.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Give two advantages of a relational database over a single flat-file database, and explain each.
question.model_answer: Less data redundancy because data is stored once and linked; and easier to maintain consistency because an update is made in one place.
question.working: Normalised tables remove duplication and update anomalies.

mark_scheme.description: Explains why (data stored once and linked)

student_answer: A relational database has less duplicated data because each piece of data is only stored once, and it is generally better and faster than a flat file.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: State one difference between a LAN and a WAN.
question.model_answer: A LAN covers a small geographic area such as one site; a WAN covers a large geographic area, often using third-party infrastructure.
question.working: Scale/geography is the key distinction.

mark_scheme.description: WAN covers a large/wide geographic area

student_answer: A LAN is over a small area like one building, but a WAN covers a much larger area like between cities or countries.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Describe a star network topology and give one advantage of using it.
question.model_answer: Each device connects to a central switch/hub; data passes through the central device. Advantage: a single cable fault only affects one device.
question.working: Central node + point-to-point links; fault isolation per link.

mark_scheme.description: Devices connect to a central switch/hub
Each device has its own connection to the centre

student_answer: In a star topology every computer is connected to a central switch by its own cable. An advantage is that if one cable breaks, only that one device loses its connection while the rest keep working.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Describe a mesh network topology and give one benefit of it.
question.model_answer: Devices are connected to many other devices so there are multiple paths for data. Benefit: if one link fails, data can take another route, so it is resilient.
question.working: Many redundant links provide alternative routes.

mark_scheme.description: Devices connect to multiple other devices
States there are multiple paths for data

student_answer: In a mesh network the devices are connected to lots of other devices, so there are many connections between them.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Explain why network protocols are organised into layers, giving two reasons.
question.model_answer: Layering breaks the task into self-contained parts so each layer can be developed and maintained independently, and layers can be changed without affecting the others as long as the interfaces stay the same.
question.working: Separation of concerns + interchangeability of layers.

mark_scheme.description: Each layer handles a self-contained part of the task
Explains interfaces between layers stay the same

student_answer: Protocols are layered because it makes the network run much faster and means more data can be sent at the same time, which improves the speed of the whole internet.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Describe how packet switching transfers data across a network.
question.model_answer: Data is split into packets; each packet is routed independently across the network and may take a different route; packets are reassembled in order at the destination.
question.working: Split -> independent routing -> reassembly.

mark_scheme.description: Packets are routed independently (possibly different routes)
Packets are reassembled at the destination

student_answer: The data is broken up into packets. Each packet is sent across the network and can go a different way, then they are put back together in the right order when they arrive.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Compare an IP address and a MAC address, covering how each is assigned and whether it changes.
question.model_answer: An IP address is assigned by the network and can change as a device moves between networks; a MAC address is set by the manufacturer and is fixed/unique to the hardware.
question.working: IP = logical, network-assigned, changeable; MAC = physical, fixed.

mark_scheme.description: IP address is assigned by the network/software
IP address can change between networks
MAC address is set by the manufacturer

student_answer: An IP address is given to a device by the network and can change if it joins a different network. A MAC address is built into the device's hardware.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Which protocol is used to request and transfer web pages? A) SMTP B) FTP C) HTTP D) IMAP
question.model_answer: C) HTTP
question.working: HTTP transfers web pages between server and browser.

student_answer: C

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Explain the purpose of the Domain Name System (DNS).
question.model_answer: DNS translates a human-readable domain name into the IP address of the server, so the request can be routed to the right machine.
question.working: Name -> IP lookup.

mark_scheme.description: Translates domain names to IP addresses
So the correct server can be located/contacted

student_answer: It is a system that helps you connect to websites on the internet.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Name three components found inside a CPU.
question.model_answer: The arithmetic logic unit (ALU), the control unit (CU) and registers (e.g. the accumulator).
question.working: Core CPU parts: ALU, CU, registers.

student_answer: The ALU, the control unit and the registers.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Describe the three stages of the fetch-decode-execute cycle.
question.model_answer: Fetch: the next instruction is copied from memory into the CPU. Decode: the control unit interprets the instruction. Execute: the instruction is carried out (e.g. by the ALU).
question.working: Three named stages with what happens in each.

mark_scheme.description: Decode: instruction interpreted by the control unit

student_answer: First the instruction is fetched from memory, then it is decoded by the CPU, and after that it is run.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: State one key feature of the Von Neumann architecture and why it matters.
question.model_answer: Programs and data are stored together in the same memory, which lets a stored program be changed without rewiring the machine.
question.working: Stored-program concept; shared memory for instructions and data.

mark_scheme.description: Programs and data share the same memory
Explains a benefit (e.g. stored programs are flexible)

student_answer: Instructions and data are both held in the same memory, which means the program stored can be changed easily rather than rebuilding the hardware.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Explain the purpose of cache memory and the effect of increasing the amount of cache.
question.model_answer: Cache stores frequently used instructions and data close to the CPU so they are accessed faster than from RAM; more cache means more data is available quickly, improving performance.
question.working: Fast nearby store reduces fetches from slower RAM.

mark_scheme.description: Faster to access than RAM (close to CPU)

student_answer: Cache holds data that is used a lot so the CPU can get to it quickly instead of fetching it from RAM each time.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Explain how clock speed affects the performance of a CPU.
question.model_answer: Clock speed is the number of cycles per second; a higher clock speed means more fetch-decode-execute cycles per second, so more instructions are processed.
question.working: More cycles/second -> more instructions/second.

mark_scheme.description: Clock speed is cycles per second
Higher speed processes more instructions per second

student_answer: The clock speed is measured in hertz and is how many cycles happen each second, so a higher clock speed lets the CPU carry out more instructions per second.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Give two differences between RAM and ROM.
question.model_answer: RAM is volatile and loses its contents when powered off, while ROM is non-volatile and keeps its contents; RAM can be read and written, while ROM is normally read-only.
question.working: Volatility and writability are the two contrasts.

student_answer: RAM is volatile so it loses its data when the power goes off, but ROM is non-volatile and keeps its data. RAM can also be changed by the computer while it runs.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Explain what virtual memory is and when it is used.
question.model_answer: Virtual memory is an area of secondary storage used as if it were RAM when RAM is full; data not currently needed is moved out to disk to free RAM.
question.working: Disk space used to extend RAM via paging when RAM is exhausted.

mark_scheme.description: Uses secondary storage as extra RAM
Data is moved between RAM and disk

student_answer: Virtual memory is a small fast type of memory inside the CPU that stores instructions the processor uses most often so it can reach them more quickly.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Compare an SSD and an HDD, covering how they store data and one practical difference.
question.model_answer: An SSD stores data on flash memory with no moving parts; an HDD stores data magnetically on spinning platters. An SSD is faster and more shock-resistant than an HDD.
question.working: Flash vs magnetic platters; SSD faster/more robust.

mark_scheme.description: SSD uses flash memory / no moving parts
HDD stores data magnetically on platters
Difference is correct (e.g. SSD faster/more durable)

student_answer: An SSD uses flash memory and has no moving parts, while an HDD stores data magnetically on spinning disks. An SSD is faster and harder to damage if dropped.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: State four functions of an operating system.
question.model_answer: Memory management, process/CPU management, peripheral/device management and user interface (also file and user account management).
question.working: Core OS roles.

student_answer: An operating system manages the memory and manages the programs running on the CPU.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Give one example of utility software and state what it does.
question.model_answer: Disk defragmentation software, which reorganises files on a disk so they are stored contiguously, speeding up access.
question.working: Any valid utility + correct purpose.

mark_scheme.description: Names a valid utility (e.g. defrag, antivirus, backup)

student_answer: Antivirus software, which scans the computer for malware and removes or quarantines anything harmful it finds.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: State what malware is and give one example.
question.model_answer: Malware is malicious software designed to damage or gain unauthorised access to a computer; an example is a virus.
question.working: Definition + a valid example.

mark_scheme.description: Malicious software intended to harm/gain access
Gives a valid example (virus, worm, trojan, etc.)

student_answer: Malware is harmful software written to damage a computer or steal data, and one example is a trojan.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Describe what phishing is and state one way a user can protect against it.
question.model_answer: Phishing is sending fake messages pretending to be from a trusted source to trick people into revealing personal data. Protection: do not click links in unexpected emails / check the sender address.
question.working: Deception to harvest data + a sensible defence.

mark_scheme.description: Fake/fraudulent messages pretending to be trusted
Aim is to obtain personal/sensitive data

student_answer: Phishing is when someone sends fake emails pretending to be a real company so that you give away your details.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Explain what an SQL injection attack is and one way to defend against it.
question.model_answer: An attacker enters SQL code into an input field so that it runs against the database and exposes or changes data. Defence: validate/sanitise inputs or use parameterised queries.
question.working: Malicious SQL via input + input handling defence.

mark_scheme.description: Malicious SQL entered via an input field
Runs against the database to read/alter data

student_answer: An SQL injection is a type of computer virus that gets into the database server through an email attachment and then deletes all the records, and you stop it by installing antivirus software.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Explain the purpose of a firewall.
question.model_answer: A firewall monitors incoming and outgoing network traffic and blocks traffic that does not meet its security rules.
question.working: Traffic filtering against rules.

mark_scheme.description: Blocks traffic against set rules / unauthorised access

student_answer: A firewall keeps a computer safe from hackers and viruses.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: For inputs A = 1 and B = 0, give the output of A AND B and the output of A OR B.
question.model_answer: A AND B = 0; A OR B = 1.
question.working: AND needs both 1 -> 0. OR needs either 1 -> 1.

student_answer: A AND B = 0 and A OR B = 1.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Evaluate the expression (A AND B) OR NOT C for A = 1, B = 0, C = 1. Show each step.
question.working: A AND B = 1 AND 0 = 0. NOT C = NOT 1 = 0. 0 OR 0 = 0.

student_answer: A AND B is 0, and NOT C is 0, so the whole thing is 0 OR 0 which I wrote down as 1.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Name two types of validation check and describe what each one does.
question.model_answer: Range check ensures a value is within set limits; length check ensures the number of characters is within an allowed range (e.g. presence/format checks also valid).
question.working: Two named checks + correct description of each.

mark_scheme.description: Names a valid check (e.g. range check)
Names a second valid check (e.g. length check)

student_answer: A range check makes sure a number is between two limits. A length check is another type of validation you can use.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Which data type is most suitable for storing whether a light is on or off? A) Integer B) String C) Boolean D) Real
question.model_answer: C) Boolean
question.working: On/off is a two-state value, best stored as a Boolean.

student_answer: C

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Explain the difference between a count-controlled (FOR) loop and a condition-controlled (WHILE) loop.
question.model_answer: A FOR loop repeats a set number of times known in advance; a WHILE loop repeats while a condition is true, so the number of iterations may not be known in advance.
question.working: Fixed count vs condition-driven repetition.

mark_scheme.description: FOR loop repeats a known/fixed number of times
WHILE loop repeats while a condition is true
WHILE iteration count may be unknown in advance

student_answer: A FOR loop runs a fixed number of times that you decide in advance, while a WHILE loop keeps running as long as a condition stays true, so you might not know beforehand how many times it loops.

question.subject: cs
question.stem: Give one benefit of using subroutines in a program and explain why.
question.model_answer: Code is easier to maintain because a piece of logic is written once and reused, so a change only needs to be made in one place.
question.working: Reuse + single point of change.

mark_scheme.description: States a valid benefit (reuse / readability / maintainability)

student_answer: They make the program shorter.
